text: A postmortem of our May 7, 2026 outage A localized AWS data center cooling failure triggered a thermal shutdown, which caused a multi-hour platform outage because Coinbase lacked automated cross-zone failover for its single-location matching engine. Coinbase is resolving this by implementing warm cross-zone standbys and migrating infrastructure to a 3-AZ deployment

text: Radiant Capital to Wind Down Operations After Failing to Recover From $50 Million Exploit Radiant Capital announced that its DAO will begin a gradual wind-down after failing to recover from the October 2024 exploit, secure new financing, or restore growth. The protocol will remain operational for withdrawals, loan repayments, and position management, while development and expansion efforts will cease. Borrowing caps will be reduced to zero and RDNT incentives will be discontinued. The exploit resulted in losses of more than $50 million. — link

text: Coinbase Releases May Outage Report: AWS Data Center Failure Caused 8-Hour Disruption, Regional Redundancy Upgrade Planned Coinbase released a postmortem on the May 7 outage that disrupted trading, deposits, withdrawals, and other core services for about eight hours, with full recovery taking roughly 12 hours. The incident was caused by the simultaneous failure of multiple cooling units at an AWS us-east-1 availability zone, which triggered server shutdowns due to overheating. Coinbase said the outage exposed shortcomings in its failover and disaster recovery systems and plans to strengthen regional redundancy, expand Kafka from two to three availability zones, and increase resilience testing. — link

text: Building index-tracking assets on top of options instead of debt Instead of borrowing against ETH collateral (like current stablecoins), split 1 ETH into a pair of synthetic options. At maturity, a slow/delayed oracle decides how the ETH is divided between the two pieces - they always add up to exactly 1 ETH, with no debt or liquidations possible Much safer (no need for fragile real-time price feeds, no forced selling during crashes), but requires regular rebalancing to keep the asset tracking the target price (e.g. $1), which creates some costs and gradual drift over time

text: Crypto Fund Outflows Hit $1.67B, Bitcoin Records Biggest Weekly Withdrawal of 2026 CoinShares reported $1.67 billion in outflows from digital asset investment products last week, marking the third consecutive week of net outflows and the second-largest weekly outflow of 2026. text: Binance Enters U.S. Stock Trading, Plans bStocks Tokenized Equities According to Fortune, Binance will allow non-U.S. users to trade more than 7,000 U.S. stocks and ETFs with zero commissions and fractional shares starting at $5. Users can purchase equities using USDT, USDC, BNB and selected cryptocurrencies, with Nest Trading providing brokerage services and Alpaca handling custody, dividends and corporate actions. Binance also plans to launch tokenized stocks, known as bStocks, in the coming weeks, allowing users to convert eligible equities into on-chain assets on BNB Chain as part of its broader push to become a "multi-asset financial super app." Binance disclosed additional details of its new U.S. stock trading service, including 24/5 trading, zero commissions, and a minimum platform fee of $0.35 per order, or 10 bps for orders above $350. The exchange also stated that its upcoming bStocks product will represent financial instruments rather than direct ownership of the underlying shares. In addition, Binance will launch Fully Paid Securities Lending (FPSL) on June 4, allowing users to lend fully paid eligible securities and earn additional yield. — link

text: Citi said in its Tokenization 2030: Wall Street On-Chain report that the real-world asset tokenization market could grow from $17 billion today to $5.5 trillion by 2030, with estimates ranging from $2.7 trillion to $8.2 trillion depending on adoption speed. Citi expects 10% of the U.S. Treasury bill market and 3% of the U.S. public stock market to be tokenized by 2030. The report also said stablecoin growth could generate about $1 trillion in new demand for U.S. Treasuries, while a 10% shift by everyday U.S. investors to digital trading platforms could create $2.6 trillion in demand for digital stocks. — link

text: BullX said in its official Discord announcement and on its website that trading in the current version of its app was paused starting June 1, 2026, at 00:00 UTC, as the team shifts resources to future development and improvements for the next version. BullX said wallet functions will continue to operate normally, while withdrawals, allocations and other wallet management features will remain available. BullX was once one of the largest meme trading tools. — link

text: Sui Foundation said three mainnet halts on Thursday and Friday were caused by two separate bugs introduced in the v1.72 upgrade. The first two halts were tied to a gas-charging bug exposed by the "address balances" feature, while a rushed interim fix carried a known low-probability halt risk that later triggered another halt. The third halt occurred when validators restarted to install the fix and hit a latent bug in how the network preserves randomness settings. Sui said the bugs have been fixed, no user funds were at risk, no settled transactions were reverted, and it has built a mechanism to force-close a stalled epoch. — link

text: Highlight Clip Robert Kiyosaki: Buffett and Schiff Are Really Stupid to Bash Bitcoin (ARCHIVE FOOTAGE) On March 5, Robert Kiyosaki said in a video that Warren Buffett and Peter Schiff have been criticizing Bitcoin, adding, "I think that's really stupid." He argued that Schiff sells gold and has an incentive to attack Bitcoin, while Buffett is too old. Kiyosaki said he likes gold, Bitcoin and Ethereum, but what he truly dislikes is the Federal Reserve, which he believes turned the U.S. into the world's largest debtor through money printing. He urged people to learn the history of the Fed, saying the real enemy is the Fed, not Bitcoin. — link
